Here's what I want to highlight though: masking is optional. The SDK docs say so plainly. It's a module you configure in the orchestration workflow. If it's not configured, it doesn't do anything.

const orchestrationClient = new OrchestrationClient({
  llm: { model_name: 'gpt-4o' },
  // masking goes HERE, if you add it
  masking: {
    masking_providers: [maskingProvider]
  }
});

In short: No masking block, no masking. SAP's own tutorials describe data masking, content filtering, and translation as "optional orchestration modules." Not defaults.

And this isn't just my read of the docs. Jana Subramanian (SAP's APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor) published a detailed security architecture overview of the two inference paths in SAP AI Core. When he describes the orchestration pipeline for LLM calls, he says the guardrails apply "where enabled." That's the same point, from SAP's own side.

The question worth asking in your landscape:

did someone enable them?

What masking catches, and what it doesn't

Say masking is configured. What does it actually catch?

The DPI service recognizes specific entities: person names, email addresses, phone numbers. Classic PII. You can define custom entities with regex, so employee IDs matching EMP-12345 can be masked too.

But think about what actually flows through an SAP system:

  • Next quarter's revenue forecast
  • A customer's contract value
  • Pricing from an ongoing negotiation
  • A strategic project codenamed "Titan"

None of that is PII. None of it triggers standard masking. But it's all commercially sensitive, and most CFOs wouldn't want it sitting on someone else's infrastructure, even transiently.

Unless you build custom regex patterns for every case, this data passes through. In practice, most implementations won't.

[Update pending: I've asked SAP AI Core Product Management directly whether commercially sensitive data beyond PII is covered. I'll update once I hear back.]

The "no training" promise

SAP confirmed that third-party LLM providers don't use your data for model training. This is handled through SAP's vendor onboarding process with standardized contracts.

The phrasing was that "opting out on the 'use data for training' is possible" through these agreements. Note the word "possible." It's careful wording.

This is a contractual guarantee, not a technical one. Your data still reaches the LLM provider's infrastructure and gets processed there. It's just not retained or used for training afterwards.

For most customers, that's fine. But my careful customer from ten years ago would probably ask: what happens if the contract changes? If the vendor gets acquired? If a subprocessor enters the chain?

Those aren't paranoid questions. They're standard DPO questions.

[Update pending: I've also asked whether this opt-out is the default for all SAP-served models, or whether each customer needs to request it.]

There are other options (and they might be enough)

Here's something I think gets lost in the Joule excitement: not every use case needs GPT-4o.

SAP AI Core distinguishes between models hosted on AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, or GCP Vertex AI (all US hyperscalers), and models that are "hosted and managed by SAP" directly. Mistral falls in the second group. So do IBM, Nvidia, and a set of open-source models.

For a lot of common tasks (summarization, classification, simple Q&A, document generation with templates) an open-source or EU-hosted model is often enough. Sometimes more than enough. And if you want to go further, you can run your own open-source model entirely on your own infrastructure or in a self-managed BTP deployment. No third-party LLM provider in the chain at all.

This is the part I'd push on if you're in a regulated industry or a Mittelstand company with strict data governance: the "use a US hyperscaler LLM" path is the default, but it's not the only path. The orchestration layer's harmonized API makes switching models a configuration change, not a rewrite.

Use what you have. Validate what you need.

[Update pending: And I've asked about inference location for EU-hosted customers. I'll add their answer here.]

What I'd actually do

If you're evaluating or already running AI features in your SAP landscape, here's my practical checklist:

  1. Ask your BTP team whether data masking is configured in the orchestration layer, or whether you're running on defaults. If they're not sure, you have your answer.
  2. Map your sensitive data beyond PII. Revenue numbers, customer names in business context, project codes, pricing. Then check if any of it is actually covered by your masking configuration.
  3. Understand the full data path. From SAP application, through the orchestration layer, to the LLM, and back. Know which model, where it's hosted, and what's contractual vs. architectural.
  4. Look at SAP-hosted or open-source models before defaulting to GPT-4o. A smaller EU-hosted model may fit your use case and your compliance requirements better.
  5. Check what your policy actually says. "No training" and "no transmission" aren't the same thing. Make sure you're meeting the real requirement.
  6. Make it an explicit decision. Whether you enable masking or skip it, document the choice. The worst outcome is a default that no one thought about.

Why now

Joule is being embedded across the SAP product suite. The Generative AI Hub is becoming a core platform capability. AI in SAP isn't experimental anymore.

That's why this conversation is worth having now instead of after everyone has enabled these features. SAP has built the building blocks for responsible AI usage. But building blocks need assembly, and right now the instructions are scattered across SDK docs, help portal pages, and conference hallway conversations.

My customer from ten years ago wouldn't host his SAC data on AWS. Today, that same level of care would probably serve a lot of organizations well. Not to block progress. Just to know what you're agreeing to when you click "enable."

Have you turned on Joule or the Generative AI Hub? What questions came up, or didn't? I'd genuinely like to hear how other teams are approaching this. 

Links:

  • SAP AI Core, Data Masking
  • SAP Data Privacy Integration
  • SAP AI Core, Security
  • Secure Data Flow for AI Inference on SAP BTP: RPT-1 vs LLM Orchestration
  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI, Orchestration
  • SAP Tutorial, Data Masking in Orchestration

entry.title: Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI
entry.content:
Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI

A working prototype and the idea behind it

IN THIS POST

  1. The scaling question
  2. The core idea
  3. The example
  4. Why this is different from Joule capabilities
  5. The architecture - KG as runtime brain
  6. Users do not think in system boundaries
  7. BDC - a potential path to faster coverage
  8. The honest complexity
  9. The prototype
  10. Closing thoughts

The scaling question

Today, building AI on SAP often means building capabilities one use case at a time. A new question introduces a new YAML definition, API binding, and deployment cycle. This model works well for many scenarios. But it raises a natural question: how does it scale as the variety and cross-system reach of user questions increases?

SAP's platform invitation is explicit: Joule as "an AI agent orchestrator," an agent builder for custom deployments, Business Data Cloud to "supercharge AI agents with the data that matters most, from any source." The infrastructure is there. This post explores one possible model for what to build on top of it - a working prototype running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, using a real A2A endpoint and a live PostgreSQL knowledge graph to answer retrieval questions without per-use-case authoring.

This is a personal exploration, not a product announcement. All views are my own. One constraint upfront: this covers read-oriented scenarios only - retrieval, explanation, analytics. Transactional write operations are out of scope.

The core idea

Joule owns the conversation. A2A owns the routing. The knowledge graph owns proven access patterns. OData owns the live data.

Each layer has one job. When a new system is added or a business term changes, only the relevant layer changes. The rest stays untouched.

This separation of concerns is the architectural backbone  and the reason the model handles question variability differently than a capability-per-use-case approach. The knowledge graph accumulates validated OData patterns over time; the A2A runtime draws on them at request time. Questions the KG already knows how to answer require no new authoring.

The example

A user says to Joule: "Show me my active purchase orders from supplier Bosch." No capability was pre-authored for that question.

Here is what happens:

 

Fig 1. The KG holds the pattern. The A2A layer matches the intent and composes the filter at runtime. Joule sees a card identical to any native capability.

The KG held the base pattern for active purchase orders. The slot extractor identified "Bosch" from the utterance and composed the OData filter at runtime.

The actual call:

The response template stored alongside the pattern in the KG rendered those records as a Joule card. The seam between Joule and the agent is invisible to the end user.

This leads to an important distinction from how Joule capabilities are typically built today.

Why this is different from Joule capabilities

SAP's Architecture Center documents two paths for extending Joule with custom agents: low-code via Joule Studio, and pro-code via the Bring Your Own Agent pattern -  where you expose an A2A-compliant HTTP endpoint and register it as a Joule Scenario. This prototype follows the pro-code path. What the knowledge graph adds is a runtime resolution layer on top of it: instead of one endpoint per use case, one endpoint resolves across all patterns the KG knows.

            TRADITIONAL JOULE CAPABILITY

             A2A + KNOWLEDGE GRAPH MODEL

  • One YAML file per use case, authored upfront
  • Hardcoded API, filter, and field selection
  • New question type = new capability version
  • Scale = more YAML
 
  • Patterns live in the KG, not in YAML files
  • Slot extraction composes filters at runtime
  • New supplier, year, or document: same pattern handles all
  • Scale = more usage
 

When to Use Each Approach

  • Traditional Joule Capabilities: Simple, well-defined use cases; single system queries; predictable user patterns
  • KG-Driven Runtime: Cross-system queries; high variation scenarios; exploratory business questions

The shift is from design-time authoring to runtime resolution. Governance changes accordingly: instead of "who owns this YAML?" the question becomes "who validated this OData pattern?" - answered by a domain lead approving a query-and-result pair, not a developer editing files.

Reference: SAP Architecture Center -  Integrating AI Agents with Joule documents the Bring Your Own Agent pro-code path, including A2A v0.3.0 protocol requirements and Joule Scenario registration.

The architecture - KG as runtime brain

Full architecture - A2A + Knowledge Graph on SAP BTP

Fig 2. End-to-end architecture: Joule routes via A2A to the self-hosted agent on BTP CF; the Knowledge Graph resolves intent and composes OData filters at runtime. Dashed boxes are planned / not yet GA.

 

 


Fig 3. The A2A layer is thin routing. The KG is where intelligence accumulates. The factory and runtime are the same flywheel - capability builds deposit patterns; the A2A runtime draws on them.

SAP's pro-code reference architecture for BTP agents also names a Knowledge Graph Engine - part of HANA Cloud - as the layer for encoding semantic business relationships. The PostgreSQL KG here is a developer-accessible analog of that concept: the same idea of accumulated, semantically enriched access patterns, running on a BTP-bound managed service rather than HANA Cloud.

Users do not think in system boundaries

"What is our total spend with supplier Bosch this year?" That question touches S/4HANA purchase orders, Ariba contracts, and Concur expense claims simultaneously. Users ask it as one question. Today it typically requires three separate lookups.

A2A offers a path toward answering it in one response - routing to specialized agents per system at runtime and composing the result. Each agent knows its own domain; none needs to know about the others. The same pattern applies across the SAP portfolio: procurement in S/4HANA, sourcing in Ariba, workforce in SuccessFactors, expense in Concur, external workforce in Fieldglass. The A2A routing layer is what makes cross-system answers possible without cross-system coupling.

One practical challenge emerges quickly: how to build initial KG coverage.

BDC - a potential path to faster coverage

The model works without BDC. But adoption will be slow. Building coverage through validated OData executions alone is a gradual process, and until the KG has enough patterns, too many intents fall back to "not yet supported."

BDC Data Products are designed to expose semantically enriched, consumption-ready models covering business definitions, domain taxonomy, and cross-entity relationships across SAP applications. If those models can be used to seed provisional KG entries, and live OData executions then promote them to validated, the cold-start problem becomes more manageable. Whether that seeding pipeline is straightforward to build depends on how BDC exposes its metadata in a given tenant, something worth exploring rather than assuming.

Raw OData tells you the shape of the data. BDC aims to tell you what the data means. That distinction could matter significantly when matching natural language to a query.

BDC also points toward a second channel - analytical questions that are difficult or impractical to answer through transactional OData alone, such as aggregated spend analysis or trend queries across periods. Those typically require pre-computed or aggregated data rather than a live API call. This is a natural conceptual extension of the same A2A routing layer, though not yet built in this prototype.

One important caveat: BDC's semantic model could provide a useful starting point, but tenant-specific customizations, restricted entity sets, and version differences would still require live validation before any pattern is trusted at runtime. BDC reduces the cold-start problem, it does not eliminate the validation step.

The honest complexity

Three areas are harder in practice than the architecture suggests.

Intent matching

PostgreSQL full-text search on intent keywords is fast but brittle on synonyms: "open orders" and "not invoiced" can mean the same thing; "cancelled" means different things in different modules. Production-grade matching would benefit from hybrid search combining full-text with embedding-based vector similarity so that semantic proximity compensates for lexical gaps.

Slot extraction

Resolving "Bosch" from a free-text utterance to a SupplierID sounds straightforward. In practice, users type partial names, aliases, or misspellings. Production-grade slot resolution needs alias handling, fuzzy matching, and potentially a lookup against master data, making it functionally a mini-MDM problem.

Authorization

The prototype authenticates via XSUAA JWT but calls OData using a service token, meaning it does not enforce row-level authorization per user. The right production path is principal propagation through BTP Destination Service - running the OData call under the user's own identity. This is on the roadmap but not yet implemented. The SAP Reference Architecture notes that the production Agent Gateway path uses IAS App2App tokens with named user context; the delta between that and a service token is the same gap this prototype still needs to close.

                                What works well

                                       Real limitations

  • Deterministic: every answer traces to a validated OData path - no hallucinated field names
  • Fast: KG lookup + OData call + render is sub-second
  • Auditable: every response traces to a specific pattern, entity, and filter
  • Composable: each system's agent is independently owned and evolvable

 

  • Cold start: BDC seeding may help; without some form of pre-seeding, early coverage is thin
  • Intent matching: full-text search alone misfires on synonyms and ambiguous phrasing
  • Slot resolution: aliases and fuzzy matching needed for production-grade accuracy
  • Authorization: row-level security requires principal propagation - not yet implemented
  • Schema drift: S/4HANA upgrades can silently break existing patterns
 

 

Governance model (in the prototype): Patterns are proposed automatically when a query succeeds. A domain lead reviews and promotes to validated=true before the pattern is trusted at runtime. Validation evidence - query, response, timestamp, user context - is logged against each pattern. Patterns not executed in N days are flagged for review, not deleted.

The prototype

Running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry: Next.js application, SAP AppRouter for XSUAA authentication, PostgreSQL knowledge graph, BTP Destination Service for OData connectivity.

 

Context: SAP's official path for custom agents connecting to Joule is the Agent Gateway - documented in the SAP Architecture Center as exposing Joule Agents via A2A 0.3.0 with IAS App2App authentication. The Agent Gateway is not yet generally available. This prototype takes the developer path in the interim: a self-hosted A2A endpoint on BTP CF using XSUAA JWT. When the Agent Gateway reaches GA, the authentication model and registration process would change - the KG and OData runtime layer underneath would not.

Fig 4. * Service token today; principal propagation (user identity through to OData) is on the roadmap. When Agent Gateway reaches GA, the entry point shifts from self-hosted AppRouter to SAP-managed endpoint - the KG and OData layers remain unchanged.

Dual protocol handler

The endpoint accepts both A2A JSON-RPC 2.0 and Joule's native agent-request format. Both converge on the same resolver:

 

The resolver: KG lookup, slot extraction, OData, render

 

The knowledge graph schema

Joule's contextId field carries pagination state across conversation turns. When a user says "show more," the skip offset and pattern ID are encoded in the context ID,  so the next turn re-runs the same OData query from the correct offset without a new KG lookup.

A note on terminology: this prototype uses "knowledge graph" loosely. What PostgreSQL holds here is more precisely a validated pattern store , flat rows with a full-text index, not a graph with typed  relationships between nodes. A true knowledge graph would add an edge table encoding relationships like A_SupplierInvoice → Procure-to-Pay → Payment, enabling process-chain traversal without LLM reasoning. The current schema is the right foundation for that - kg_services, kg_entities, kg_patterns are already the node types. The edges are what's missing. That gap is intentional for now: the pattern store is enough to prove the runtime resolution model. The graph layer is the next honest step.

Closing thoughts

The pieces fit together reasonably well. The A2A protocol is documented and implemented in Joule. The OData catalog is rich and well-governed. BTP handles authentication and connectivity. The engineering gaps - vector search, slot resolution, principal propagation are real but well-defined.

For teams building on Joule and A2A today, this suggests a possible shift in focus: from defining individual capabilities to curating reusable, validated access patterns. The two approaches are not mutually exclusive ; capability builds are what populate the KG in the first place. But the longer-term value may compound differently when patterns are shared rather than siloed per use case.

The idea to sit with

This is not a replacement for Joule capabilities. It is a complementary layer - one that may make them more scalable over time.

The real question is not can this work?
It is: who owns the knowledge graph?

One possible direction for SAP AI is not simply more capabilities, but fewer, more adaptive ones backed by a shared knowledge layer that agents draw on at runtime. SAP is delivering the ingredients: Joule as orchestrator, A2A as the routing standard, BDC as the semantic foundation.

Who else is exploring this direction?

This post reflects a personal exploration and learning exercise building on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. All views are my own and do not represent SAP's positions, roadmap, or product direction. Code snippets are from a personal prototype and are shared for illustrative purposes only.

Co-Authored with J4C and Sonnet 🙂

entry.title: SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk 2605 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk (SM&R) Community!

If you're interested in learning about some of the new features coming with our May release, you're in the right place. 

Let's take a look at what's new within SM&R this quarter...

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance

Additional Authorizations for AI Copilot Joule Scenarios in Supplier Management (SLP-2412)

This feature refines the requirements for user group membership to see prompts for specific Joule supplier management scenarios. This refinement ensures that prompts related to specific aspects of supplier management functionality are only visible  to users with relevant permissions, in customer landscapes that include the applicable solutions.

 

 

 

Ability to Restrict the Number of Commodity, Region, and Department Answers in External Questionnaires (SLP-2382)

This feature enables you to configure the maximum number of answers process initiators can add to Commodity, Region and Department questions in the external modular questionnaire.

Process initiators can only add the maximum number of answers you specify when creating a process, ensuring that processes meet your business requirements. It is applicable for external modular questionnaire. If the data is pre-populated from the registration stage or initial values specified from the template, the selected items need to be within the defined levels in the template configuration. If the pre-populated answers exceed the specified range, you must remove the values that are outside of the defined levels in the template configuration. 

 

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Risk

Additional Joule Capabilities in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk (SRISK-1860)

Your AI copilot Joule integrates with the Risk Exposure API - making it that much faster to find information via natural language prompting.

Take a look at these examples: 

 

Enhanced Virus scanning capability in Supplier Risk (SRISK-1329)

This feature automatically scans all files uploaded during different stages of an engagement request for viruses and malware, providing enhanced security protection throughout the supplier onboarding process. When documents are uploaded during any stage of creating an engagement request, each file is scanned in real-time before the next upload can begin. This sequential upload process ensures thorough security screening of documents.

Previously, you could upload multiple files consecutively without interruption. The system now requires a scan to complete before you can upload the next file. Please allow a brief moment between file uploads.

 

 

 

Enhancing Risk Exposure API by Adding Standard and Custom Category in Response (SRISK-1856)

 This feature enables the Risk Exposure API to retrieve responses for both standard and custom risk categories for suppliers you monitor in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk. The responses depend on the parameter setting for Enable custom risk categories (Application.SR.Risk.EnableCustomRiskCategories) on your site.

The current version (v3) of Risk Exposure API returns responses regardless of the parameter setting. 

When the Enable custom risk categories parameter is enabled, the API returns data for your custom risk categories.

When the parameter is disabled, the API automatically returns data for the default risk categories: Environmental & Social, Financial, Operational, and Regulatory & Legal.

(The previous version (v2) of the Risk Exposure API returned responses only when the Enable custom risk categories parameter was enabled.)

 

Once these features are officially released (May 15th) you can locate additional implementation and technical details, by simply searching for the feature ID here: What's New (via SAP Help Portal)

 

 

​Stay tuned for our additional upcoming blog posts and follow me to stay informed on upcoming feature releases across SAP Ariba.

​​Thank you for being a part of the SAP Ariba community and we look forward to continuing our journey of innovation together!

 

 

​​

 

Resources:

What’s New - where you can find implementation and Technical Details for any of these features (once released on May 15)

SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)

Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)

SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page

SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network

entry.title: How We Built JUNO Joule Agent for Release Testing Automation Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

From my experience working on multiple SAP SuccessFactors release cycles, one thing became very clear early on — automation alone is not enough. What really makes a difference is how intelligently we manage and execute that automation.

In most projects, teams already invest a lot of effort in building automated test scenarios using internal frameworks or QA tools. But the real challenge starts during release time — deciding what to run, when to run it, and how to interpret the results.

I’ve personally seen teams struggle with simple but critical things like:

  • Manually triggering test executions
  • Switching between tools to track execution status
  • Trying to understand raw test results
  • Mapping technical failures back to business impact

Even though automation exists, the process still feels manual and fragmented.

This gap is exactly what led us to build JUNO Joule Agent — to make release testing not just automated, but also intelligent and easy to manage.    

Current Challenge

In most SAP SuccessFactors implementations I’ve worked on, organizations already have:

  • Automated test scenarios built in their release testing frameworks
  • CI/CD pipelines capable of triggering executions

But what’s usually missing is:

  • A simple, business-friendly way to trigger tests
  • Smart selection of relevant test scenarios per release
  • Clear visibility into execution results
  • A guided or conversational way to validate releases

Because of this, even though testing is technically automated, the overall process still depends heavily on manual effort.

Our Approach – JUNO Joule Agent

To solve this problem, we designed JUNO Joule Agent, an AI-powered release testing solution built on SAP Business Technology Platform.

While working on this, my main focus was to ensure the solution feels natural within the SAP ecosystem — secure, scalable, and easy for both technical and business users.

As part of the implementation, we built the solution using SAP Build Process Automation, leveraging Joule Actions, Skills, and Agent capabilities to orchestrate the entire release testing flow. This helped us connect user requests directly with backend automation in a seamless way.

Instead of creating new test scripts again, JUNO reuses existing automated test scenarios from the release testing framework and brings everything into one place.

With this approach, teams can trigger test execution using simple natural language, monitor execution in real time, and analyze results without switching across multiple tools.

It basically adds an intelligent layer on top of existing automation, which is what was missing earlier.

Benefits

1. Centralized Test Dashboard

  • Displays a unified dashboard to execute and monitor test scenarios, along with options to export results.
  • Provides visibility into test scenarios and execution status across different modules.

 

 Figure 1: JUNO Joule Agent showing list of configured Joule Skills, Agents and release testing artifacts

 

2. Intelligent Test Execution via Joule

  • Accepts natural language user requests and identifies the corresponding release test scenario.

  • Triggers test execution directly from the conversation and provides execution summary and monitoring details.

 

Figure 2:  Natural Language-Based Test Execution using Joule with real-time execution summary and monitoring details

 

Figure 3: Real-time test result insights using Joule showing passed and failed scenario counts

 

3. Faster and More Efficient Testing

  • From what I observed during implementation, this significantly reduced repetitive manual testing effort and helped teams complete release cycles faster with fewer errors.

  • It also allowed HR and IT teams to focus more on analysis and improvements instead of spending time on manual execution.

Figure 4: Triggering multiple release test scenarios using Joule with execution status and monitoring details

 

4. Release Highlights Available in the UI

  • Displays SAP SuccessFactors release updates directly within the application.

  • Shows key changes and related information in a structured format for quick reference.

 5. Testing Across SAP SuccessFactors HCM Modules

  • Supports release testing across multiple SAP SuccessFactors modules, including Employee Central, Onboarding, and Recruiting.

  • Retrieves available test scenarios across these modules using Joule.

 

Figure 5:  Conversational retrieval of Employee Central data using Joule with real-time response display

 

Figure 6: Detailed view of Time Off test cases and execution insights retrieved through Joule conversational interface

 

Figure 7: Conversational view of release RCM test cases and associated scenario details using Joule

6. Test Scenario Execution Using Inputs

  • Allows users to run test scenarios for a specific Entity ID instead of executing all scenarios.

  • Executes only the relevant test cases based on the provided input.

 

Figure 8: Input-driven hierarchical test scenario execution using Joule with detailed execution summary

 

Figure 9: Triggering release test scenarios using Joule with execution success status and details

 7. Release Test Coverage Across SAP SuccessFactors Modules

  • Displays the number of automated test scenarios available for each SAP SuccessFactors HCM module.
  • Retrieves test scenario counts across modules using Joule.

 

Figure 10: Conversational retrieval of test scenario counts across all SAP SuccessFactors modules using Joule

 

Figure 11: Real-time insight into average execution time of release test scenarios using Joule

 

Business Value

From my hands-on experience, JUNO brings clear improvements in how release testing is handled:

- Makes the release testing process more structured and easier to manage across different modules
- Significantly reduces manual effort by enabling test execution through natural language and centralized monitoring
- Ensures consistent and repeatable testing, helping avoid missed validations during release cycles
- Minimizes the risk of critical issues by improving test coverage and visibility
- Improves collaboration between HR and IT teams through clear and accessible execution insights
- Runs securely on SAP Business Technology Platform and scales effectively across multiple modules
- In my experience, this also improved overall confidence during release cycles, as teams had better visibility and control over test execution

Conclusion

Release testing in SAP SuccessFactors is critical, but managing it can become complex with frequent updates and multiple dependencies.

From what I’ve seen in real projects, adding an intelligent layer like JUNO Joule Agent completely changes the way teams approach testing. Instead of worrying about tools, scripts, or execution steps, teams can directly focus on validating business scenarios.

For me, the biggest impact was the significant reduction in manual effort, improved clarity across the testing process, and overall smoother and faster release cycles.

This approach not only improves efficiency but also changes how teams think about release testing—from a technical task to a more business-focused validation process.

As a consultant, you will gain expert-level, up-to-date knowledge, empowering you to deliver tangible business value to your clients.

  • Troubleshoot common setup issues and stay current with Joule updates and resources.
  • Extend Joule’s value to experience platforms and external environments securely.

Start Learning: Implementing Joule Across your Organizational System Landscape

entry.title: SAP Joule Document Grounding: What I Learned the Hard Way
entry.content:
I'll be honest: when I was asked to set up SAP Joule Document Grounding in SuccessFactors for our HR business, I was excited but also a little nervous. It was my first time working on SAP's AI capabilities, and the idea of connecting Joule to our actual company documents felt like a big step. The official documentation made it look straightforward enough. Spoiler: it wasn't. 😁
What followed was a week of troubleshooting, a lot of curl errors, a few moments of genuine confusion, and eventually, a working setup. This post is my attempt to save you some of that time.

The Issues I Hit (and How I Fixed Them):

Runtime Environment: pick "Other", not Cloud Foundry

First mistake I made was selecting Cloud Foundry as the runtime when creating the Document Grounding service instance. 😅It looks like the natural choice if you're used to working on BTP, but it generates the wrong type of credentials: the mTLS endpoint you need simply won't be there.

Fix: Always choose Other for both the Document Grounding instance and the Cloud Identity Services instance. Yes, BTP will warn you that it can't bind natively in CF... that's fine, ignore it.

Windows curl will not work. Full stop.

This one cost me a few hours. I kept running into this:

curl: (58) schannel: Failed to import cert file doc-grounding.crt

The curl.exe that comes with Windows uses the Schannel TLS library, which simply doesn't handle PEM-format certificates the way we need it to. I tried converting to PFX, I tried PowerShell's Invoke-WebRequest, nothing worked cleanly. 

Fix: Download a portable curl build with OpenSSL from curl.se/windows . No installation needed, just unzip it somewhere and always call it by its full path:

C:\curl\bin\curl.exe --cert doc-grounding.crt --key doc-grounding.key ...

Once I switched to this, the certificate errors disappeared completely.

The certificate files have "\n" instead of real line breaks.

After saving the certificate and key from the BTP service binding JSON, I assumed they were ready to use. They weren't. The values in the JSON use \n as a literal escape sequence,  not actual newlines. A PEM file with \n in it isn't a valid PEM file.

Fix: Run this in PowerShell after saving each file:

(Get-Content -Path doc-grounding.crt -Raw) -replace '\\n', "`n" |
    Set-Content -Path doc-grounding.crt -Force

Do the same for the .key file. After this, -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- will be on its own line, as it should be.

PowerShell adds an invisible BOM to your JSON files.

This one is particularly sneaky because you can't see it:

{"error": {"code": 400, "message": "Unexpected UTF-8 BOM"}}

PowerShell's Out-File adds a UTF-8 Byte Order Mark by default, and the Document Grounding API doesn't like it. The fix is to write files differently:

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText(
    "C:\path\to\body.json",
    '{"type":"MSSharePoint","configuration":{...}}',
    [System.Text.Encoding]::ASCII
)

Then pass it to curl with --data "@C:\path\to\body.json".

Asterisks in cron expressions will break PowerShell.

If you try to pass a cron expression like 0 * * * * directly in a PowerShell command, things go sideways fast, PowerShell interprets * as a wildcard. The error messages you get are confusing and have nothing to do with cron.
Fix: Write the full JSON body to a file (see the previous point) instead of passing it inline. This sidesteps the wildcard issue entirely.

401 from Microsoft Graph: check three things.

If the pipeline creation goes through but you get 401 errors when it actually tries to read from SharePoint, there are three things to check in Azure AD:

  • The permission (Sites.Read.All) must be Application type, not Delegated
  • Admin Consent must be granted tenant-wide: per-user consent is not enough for a service account
  • The Client ID in the BTP Destination must be the Application (client) ID from the app's Overview page, not the Secret ID (which is a completely different GUID shown in Certificates & secrets)

That last one tripped us up, the Secret ID and the Client ID look the same format but are completely different values.

SharePoint's hidden libraries will get indexed and your users can't open them.

This one surprised me. Users were getting answers from Joule that referenced documents they couldn't open. "Access Denied." Not a great experience.
What was happening: when you don't specify includePaths, the pipeline indexes everything in the SharePoint site, including system libraries that are hidden from regular users. The most problematic one we encountered was the Preservation Hold Library (/PreservationHoldLibrary), a Microsoft 365 compliance feature that stores retained copies of modified or deleted documents. Users can't see it in SharePoint, but the indexing service account can and does.
My first thought was to add an exclusion list. Unfortunately, the Document Grounding API doesn't have an excludePaths parameter. You can only specify what to include.

A Limitation Worth Knowing Before You Go Live
I want to be upfront about something that isn't obvious from the documentation and has real implications for how you design your deployment.
Document Grounding does not respect individual user permissions in SharePoint.
Here's how it actually works: the pipeline uses a system-level service account to index documents. All that content goes into a shared vector store. When any user asks Joule a question, Joule searches that shared store, it doesn't check whether that specific user has permission to read those documents in SharePoint.
In practice, this means: if a document is indexed, every Joule user can potentially receive its content in an answer, regardless of what their SharePoint permissions say.
For our HR use case, this was something we had to think carefully about. If your SharePoint site contains documents with different access levels — say, management-only documents in the same library as general staff documents — Document Grounding will treat them all the same.
What you can do about it:

Use includePaths to only index folders where every document is appropriate for every Joule user — this is the most practical control available right now
Consider creating a dedicated SharePoint site that contains only content approved for all staff, rather than indexing your main site
Before adding any folder to your pipeline, ask yourself: "Would I be comfortable if any Joule user saw this?"

This is a known architectural constraint of the current version. Joule Document Grounding in SuccessFactors - Role-Based Access Control at Retrieval Level 

Fix: Be explicit with includePaths from the start. List only the folders that contain documents your users are actually allowed to open. Yes, it's a bit more work to maintain, but it prevents this whole category of problem.

The Tool That Made Everything Easier.

After going through all of the above, I realized there was another gap: there is currently no SAP-provided UI to monitor your Document Grounding pipelines. You can create a pipeline and walk away, but if you want to check its status, see which documents were indexed, or trigger a manual run, you have two options: call the REST API directly using a tool like Bruno (a great open-source API client), or build something yourself.
I went with the second option and built a PowerShell interactive menu script to manage the whole Document Grounding lifecycle without having to remember curl commands and JSON structures every time.

The script handles all the certificate and token complexity automatically, you just pick a number. A few things I found particularly useful in practice:
Option 5 — Get Pipeline Documents fetches the full list of indexed documents with automatic pagination, shows a breakdown by status (SUCCESS / FAILED / SKIPPED), and can export everything to a JSON file on your desktop. This is invaluable for verifying what actually got indexed after a pipeline run.
Option 8 — Create Pipeline comes pre-loaded with our production includePaths list, so there's no risk of accidentally indexing the wrong folders when setting up a new pipeline.
Option 9 — Trigger uses the correct endpoint and body format we discovered through all the troubleshooting above.

It was a fun challenge overall 🙂 my first proper dive into SAP's AI capabilities, and definitely not the last. If you're setting up Document Grounding and hit something not covered here, drop a comment. Happy to help.

  • SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer: Validate your expertise and showcase your skill set with this in-demand certification, tailored for today’s data-driven environment.

Explore More: Expert-Led Sessions & Learning Journeys

Our Artificial Intelligence Featured Learning Page is your one-stop shop to discover the full spectrum of AI offerings.

entry.title: Something Exciting is on the Horizon: Your Sapphire 2026 Session Guide
entry.content:
If you have not already, check out Mark Wright's blog post on What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development — it sets the stage perfectly for what is coming.

Sapphire 2026  is shaping up to be one of the most exciting yet. This year, we will be unveiling a wave of new AI features that our team has been developing behind the scenes. We cannot wait to share them with you.

Whether you are just getting started with SAP Build and Joule Studio or you are already an expert, there is something here for you. Our sessions are designed to meet you where you are — giving you the insights, demos, and hands-on learning to take your skills to the next level.

Join us at Sapphire 2026 — register for the format that works best for you:

  • SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando
  • SAP Sapphire Virtual
  • SAP Sapphire Madrid

 

If you are planning to visit SAP Sapphire in person, stop by our live event demo booth to meet the team and see innovation in action. Discover how SAP Build helps teams create AI-powered solutions—from extensions to workflows and agentic apps—so you can deliver faster with more agility and less complexity. Do not miss our Agent Lab experience, where you can take part in a hands-on session and see just how easy it is to build AI agents that streamline and simplify your daily work:

  • Orlando station (BTP2301) and Agent Lab Experience Hub (EXP2788)
  • Madrid station (BTP1408) and Agent Lab Experience Hub (EXP1537)

 

To help you plan your agenda, we have also curated a selection of must-attend sessions. These highlight the latest AI capabilities for agent-based applications and workflow development, bringing them to life through real-world examples.

One session, however, stands out as essential: “The Enterprise Path to Agentic AI” (BTP1454). Presented by Michael Gall, Sid Misra, and Corey Swenson, this session explores how Joule Studio enables the creation of AI solutions ranging from simple extensions to fully agentic applications. It also highlights SAP’s strategy for delivering an enterprise-grade, intent-to-execution platform tailored for SAP customers. Attendees will see how the Joule solution provides a unified, guided experience — leading developers from initial intent through blueprinting and agent specification to testing and deployment.

entry.title: Product-Agent Fit: A Practical Framework for Building on SAP BTP in the Age of Joule
entry.content:
A practical framework for SAP architects, developers, and product managers navigating the agentic enterprise

The Question SAP Is Already Asking

At SAP TechEd 2025 in Berlin, SAP Executive Board Member Muhammad Alam, in charge of SAP Product & Engineering, said SAP was entering “the era of agentic AI, where AI moves from being just a tool to becoming your trusted teammate."

A few months later, on March 31, 2026, SAP CEO Christian Klein went further in his blog: "Software is becoming the operating system for trusted autonomy." (Klein, March: “Software is Entering Its Most Powerful Era”)

These are not marketing statements. They signal architectural direction — and they have direct implications for every SAP developer, consultant, and product manager building on BTP today.

Here is the question they force us to ask about every application we build or extend:

Not only "will a user love this?" — but also "will Joule reach for it?"

That stretch — from human satisfaction to agent invocability — is what I want to explore in this post through a framework I have been developing: Product-Agent Fit (PAF).

 

What Is Product-Agent Fit?

For nearly two decades, the dominant lens for evaluating software products has been Product-Market Fit (PMF) — the degree to which a product satisfies a human market's needs well enough that users adopt it, pay for it, and recommend it. PMF assumes the validator of product value is always a human being with preferences, emotions, and tolerance for rough edges.

Product-Agent Fit (PAF) proposes an additional validator: the AI agent.

PAF is the degree to which a product's capability surface is naturally consumable by AI agents — such that agents discover it autonomously, invoke it correctly, trust its outputs, chain it into workflows, and operate within enterprise governance constraints — without human mediation at invocation time.

In SAP terms: does your CAP application, your Fiori app, your S/4HANA extension — does it work when Joule calls it? Not when a user clicks through it. When an agent invokes it.

The answer to that question is increasingly one of the most important singlas you would know about the long-term relevance of whatever you are building.

 

Why This Matters Specifically on SAP BTP

SAP has been executing a clear, multi-year trajectory toward agent-first architecture:

  • September 2023: Joule launched as a copilot — human-facing, UI-adjacent
  • October 2024: Joule went agentic — multi-agent orchestration, coordinating "across business functions" (TechEd 2024)
  • April 2025: SAP joined Google as a founding industry contributor to the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol — standardizing how agents communicate across enterprise platforms
  • November 2025: SAP unveiled major advancements in agentic AI, including introducing new Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers at TechEd 2025, enabling Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, and Windsurf to access SAP development frameworks directly
  • March 2026: Christian Klein: "Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

The direction is unambiguous. SAP is rebuilding its platform so that Joule — and agents beyond Joule — can consume every capability without depending on screen navigation.

The question for us as builders is: are the things we build on BTP keeping pace with that direction?

 

The PAF Framework — Mapped to SAP

I propose eleven dimensions for evaluating whether an SAP BTP application has achieved Product-Agent Fit. Each maps to specific SAP tools and patterns you can act on today.

Two SAP resources serve as the primary references throughout this framework. For agent-layer implementation — Joule Studio, MCP tooling, agent architecture, and the AI development lifecycle — SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the dedicated guide. For platform security, CAP patterns, ORD, and audit logging — the SAP BTP Developer's Guide applies. Where a dimension touches both layers, both are called out.

1. Capability Fit

Are your capabilities atomic enough for an agent to invoke directly?

In SAP terms: your OData V4 services, your CAP action handlers, your ABAP logic exposed as OData V4 services via RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) — are they scoped at the level of a single, invocable agent action? Not "manage the purchase order" but release_purchase_order(po_id, approver_id).

The 2,100+ pre-delivered Joule skills SAP has published by the end of 2025 are each scoped exactly this way. If you are building custom extensions, your capability granularity should match that standard.

SAP tool: CAP action definitions, OData V4 services built on RAP

2. Interface Fit

Is your API surface natively machine-readable without human explanation?

This is where MCP comes in. At TechEd 2025, SAP announced that developers using agentic coding tools can now access SAP development frameworks via SAP Build local MCP Servers. The open-source SAP MDK MCP Server (github.com/SAP/mdk-mcp-server) exposes Mobile Development Kit tools as MCP tools consumable by authorized MCP-compatible agents.

For your custom BTP application, Interface Fit means: is there a machine-readable, typed schema that describes what your service does, what parameters it takes, and what errors it returns — in a form an LLM can reason over without a human explaining it?

SAP tools: MCP Server definitions, OData V4 with typed EDM schemas, SAP AI SDK (Javascript: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-js; Java: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-java)

3. Reliability Fit

Are your outputs deterministic enough for agents to chain without verification overhead?

Christian Klein stated this precisely in his March 2026 blog post: "orchestration, policy enforcement, and workflow determinism are the gatekeepers of trust."

In practice, this means your business logic — especially anything involving financial calculations, inventory, or compliance — must be deterministic and auditable. Joule should call your service and trust the result. It should not need to re-verify the output or ask a human to confirm before passing it to the next step.

This is the separation-of-concerns principle that CAP embodies, now applied to agent consumption: your service layer handles determinism; Joule handles the reasoning. Do not mix these responsibilities.

SAP tool: CAP handlers with deterministic business logic, CDS-defined validation rules

4. Context Fit

Can Joule determine when and how to invoke your service from its schema alone?

SAP's Knowledge Graph (announced TechEd 2024) maps "relationships and context across SAP's vast data landscape" using fifty years of business process expertise — so Joule knows the semantics of your business domain, not just the syntax of your API.

For your custom application, this means: tool descriptions must be semantically rich enough for an LLM to correctly select your tool from a catalog of hundreds. A tool named processMaterial with no description will not be selected. A tool named check_material_availability with the description "Returns current stock level and ATP date for a given material in a specified plant" will be.

SAP tools: MCP tool descriptions, Joule Studio skill definitions, CDS annotations

5. Composability Fit

Does your service output feed directly into the next Joule step without transformation?

SAP's industry partnership in the A2A protocol (April 2025) is a direct investment in this dimension. A2A is intended to enable Joule to coordinate with agents from Google, Microsoft, and other vendors — "sharing context and acting together across enterprise platforms without requiring manual system switching or data loss."

For your application: every service response should be structured JSON that the next tool in the chain can consume directly. If your response is only a formatted HTML string or a PDF attachment, Joule cannot chain it — a human must intervene. That is a composability failure.

SAP tools: Structured CDS projections, A2A-compliant agent registration, Joule Studio orchestration

6. Headless Deployment Fit

Does your application deliver full value without a human opening a browser?

SAP repositioned Joule from AI copilot to "AI orchestrator across your entire organization" (February 2025) — meaning Joule should be able to execute complete workflows end-to-end, not just assist a human who is already on a screen.

Ask this about your application: if I remove the Fiori UI entirely, can Joule still accomplish the core business task? If the answer is no — because some step requires a human to click Confirm in the UI — that is a Headless Deployment Fit gap.

The fix is not to remove the UI. It is to ensure that human confirmation steps are exposed as structured approval actions that agents can surface through conversation, not as UI buttons that block the workflow.

SAP tools: CAP action handlers callable by MCP, Joule-invocable approval flows on SAP Build Work Zone

7. Trust Pipeline Fit

Does your application enforce governance at the API layer, not just the UI layer?

Walter Sun, SVP and Global Head of AI at SAP, has been direct: "accountability must always rest with humans or legal entities, never with the AI itself" (Sun, June 2025: “From Assistive AI to Agentic AI: Risks, Responsibilities, and the Road Ahead”). And Christian Klein identifies "validation rules, compliance checks, approval flows, identity management and audit trails" as non-negotiable requirements for enterprise agents (Klein, March 2026).

In practice, this means authorization enforcement must happen at your CAP service layer — not just at the Fiori UI. For new applications, this applies whether you are using XSUAA or the newer SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) and Authorization Management Service (AMS) stack that the SAP BTP Developer's Guide now recommends; the governance requirement is identical under both. An agent that bypasses the UI must encounter the same authorization gates as a human user. Every agent-invoked write should be logged to the SAP Audit Log Service with full provenance.

If your authorization logic lives only in the Fiori app and your CAP services are essentially open to any authenticated caller, you have a Trust Pipeline Fit gap — and a serious security risk in an agentic world.

SAP tools: Authorization enforcement in CAP handlers, SAP Audit Log Service, SAP Authorization and Trust Management

8. Longitudinal Context Fit

Does your application give Joule access to accumulated business knowledge, not just current-state data?

SAP's Business Data Cloud creates "a single trusted data layer" across SAP and non-SAP sources. The SAP Knowledge Graph leverages "50-plus years of business process expertise" to give agents semantic understanding of business relationships that raw data cannot provide.

For your custom application: does Joule know that this supplier has had three late deliveries in the past six months? Does it know that this customer's contract expires in 30 days? Does it understand the regulatory history of this tax position? If that context is locked in a data warehouse that Joule cannot query, your application cannot achieve longitudinal context fit.

SAP tools: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine (for RAG), SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine, SAP Analytics Cloud, CDS-defined analytical views

9. Surface-Agnostic Rendering Fit

Does your application reach the human wherever they are, not only inside Fiori?

Joule has been extended beyond SAP applications into third-party applications via WalkMe integration (SAP Sapphire, 2025), and Slack adoption for SAP agent interactions has grown significantly. The underlying principle is the same one Salesforce articulates for headless commerce and CRM — that the engagement surface can change (mobile app, Slack, Teams, voice interface) while the underlying business platform stays consistent: "The surface changes. The platform doesn't."

For your application: can the agent surface your workflow outcomes as a Slack notification, a Teams card, a mobile push alert — without you having to build a separate integration for each? If your application only surfaces results in Fiori, you are missing the engagement surfaces where your users actually spend their time.

SAP tools: SAP Build Work Zone, Joule in Slack integration, SAP Mobile Services, SAP BTP notification services

10. Agent Operational Maturity Fit

Can you detect and fix Joule behavioral drift without waiting for a user complaint?

SAP ships the LeanIX AI Agent Hub — dashboards showing agent deployment, process impact, and performance metrics. This is the SAP-side equivalent of what Salesforce calls its Testing Center and Observability tooling.

The critical insight from the industry: agents are probabilistic, not deterministic. There is no bug to fix — only behavior to observe and tune. If your Joule skill works correctly today but drifts after the next model update or business rule change, you need operational tooling to detect that drift before it affects production processes.

SAP tools: LeanIX AI Agent Hub, SAP AI Core evaluation service, Joule Studio agent testing

11. Marketplace Discovery Fit

Is your application discoverable by agents, not just by humans browsing the SAP Marketplace?

ORD — Open Resource Discovery is SAP's answer to this dimension. Now governed by the Linux Foundation Europe and NeoNephos Foundation (open-resource-discovery.org), ORD enables applications to "self-describe their exposed resources and capabilities" in a standardized, decentralized format. When your BTP application publishes ORD metadata, Joule can discover and compose your capabilities without a developer manually wiring the integration.

If your application is not publishing ORD metadata, it is invisible to the next generation of agent-based orchestration — regardless of how well it works when a human finds it manually.

SAP tools: ORD specification implementation in CAP, SAP Business Accelerator Hub publication, Joule Studio as a unified catalog and registration system 

 

The SAP PAF Maturity Ladder

Where does your application sit today?

Level 0 — INVISIBLE

  UI-only. No OData/CAP API. Joule cannot reach it.

  Example: Legacy Excel-based processes, classic Dynpro without API layer.

 

Level 1 — REACHABLE

  OData API exists but no ORD metadata, no MCP tools defined.

  Joule can be manually wired but won't discover it autonomously.

  Example: Standard CAP app without ORD publication.

 

Level 2 — CALLABLE

  OData V4 with typed schemas.

 Joule can invoke with explicit developer instruction. Errors are structured.

  Example: CAP app following the SAP BTP Developer's Guide, ORD partially configured.

 

Level 3 — DISCOVERABLE

  ORD metadata published. MCP tools defined in line with the SAP AI Golden Path.

  Joule Studio or other agent tooling can discover and wire the capability with much less manual effort.

  Example: Full SAP BTP Developer's Guide + SAP AI Golden Path + MCP + ORD.

 

Level 4 — PREFERRED

  Joule selects autonomously. Authorization enforced at CAP layer.

  Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval flows agent-accessible.

  Longitudinal context available via Business Data Cloud.

  Registered as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric / BTP agent runtime.

  Example: Production Joule Agent with full BTP governance.

 

Level 5 — FOUNDATIONAL

  Other Joule agents and third-party agents depend on yours as a building block.

  Discoverable within Joule Studio without manual wiring.

  Example: What SAP's own HANA Cloud and Master Data services are candidates to become.

 

What to Do This Sprint

With your application's level on the PAF Maturity Ladder identified, here is a concrete sprint-level action plan for each transition.

Moving from Level 1 to Level 2: Publish your CAP service with a typed OData V4 schema. Define structured error responses using CAP's error handling framework. Ensure every action has a meaningful, LLM-readable description in your CDS annotations.

Moving from Level 2 to Level 3: Implement ORD metadata in your CAP application. Define at least one MCP tool wrapping your most-used CAP action. SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the recommended starting point for MCP tool definitions, Joule Studio registration, and the agent development lifecycle. Test that Joule can invoke your tool without a human explaining the parameters, with structured errors and audit trace (in natural language).

Moving from Level 3 to Level 4: Move authorization enforcement into your CAP service handlers — not just Fiori app routing. Add Audit Log Service integration for every agent-invoked write. Design your human approval flows as CAP actions that return structured confirmation tokens, not (just) as UI buttons. Register your agent as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric or the BTP agent runtime to enable cross-vendor agent interoperability.

Moving from Level 4 to Level 5: Document your MCP tool definitions in a format LLMs can parse. Publish relevant APIs, packages, or reusable assets where appropriate. Consider submitting your Joule skill to the SAP partner ecosystem so other agent stacks can discover and reuse your capability.

 

The Framing That Changes How You Build

I want to close with Christian Klein's formulation, because I think it is the most important framing for every SAP practitioner today:

"Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

That sentence captures the challenge precisely. The hard part is not building the agent. The hard part is making your platform ready for the agent — so that when Joule arrives at your CAP service, your OData endpoint, your approval workflow, it finds something it can invoke, trust, govern, and chain.

That readiness is Product-Agent Fit.

And based on the trajectory of SAP's own platform — A2A, MCP, ORD, Joule Studio, LeanIX AI Agent Hub — it is clear that SAP is building many PAF-like requirements into the platform layer. The question is whether the applications we build on top of that platform are keeping pace.

Over to You

I am curious where the SAP community currently stands on this:

  • Are you already designing your BTP applications with Joule invocability in mind, or is agent consumption still a future concern?
  • Has your team published ORD metadata or defined MCP tools yet?
  • Where do most of your current applications sit on the PAF Maturity Ladder?

Drop your thoughts in the comments. The more specific the better — I would love to hear concrete patterns, blockers, and solutions from those already building at the frontier.

entry.title: Adapting SAP Joule for Purchase Requisition Creation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Hello, and thank you for taking the time to read this blog post!

As a member of the SAP S/4HANA RIG (Regional Implementation Group), our mission is to share collective insights and hands‑on experience with the broader SAP community through blog posts and SAP Learning courses. To date, we have published more than 700 blog posts and delivered numerous learning offerings to help customers successfully navigate SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

Today, I would like to show how you can leverage SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA and, more importantly, how you can adapt it to your specific business needs. While this example focuses on Purchase Requisition (PR) creation, the same principles can be applied to many other business processes.

SAP Joule and Purchase Requisition Creation

As you can see in the video below, as of May 2026, Joule can guide an end user through the creation of a Purchase Requisition. Additional details are available in the SAP Help Portal here: Create Purchase Requisitions.

 

This capability is already very useful for many customers. However, we also know that every customer has unique requirements. After presenting this process, I frequently received the following questions:

  • Can Joule create a Purchase Requisition using our own document type?
  • Can Joule populate additional standard fields, such as the PR Description?
  • I created a custom field in the F1481 - Custom fields app and enabled it in the respective Purchase requisition API. Could that custom field be included in the prompt?
  • Our team only creates PRs for materials. Can Joule assume I will request a material and skip the initial question?
  • Is it possible to request a PR that includes all the required information in a single prompt?
  • Can multiple materials be included directly during PR creation, rather than as a follow‑up step?

The short answer to all of these questions is yes—by leveraging Joule Studio through Agents and Skills.

As part of the SAP Build portfolio, Joule Studio allows you to tailor Joule to your specific business requirements using an intuitive, user-friendly interface. It enables you to extend standard capabilities, connect to existing SAP and customer-specific services, and guide how Joule responds based on business context and input.

In addition, Joule Studio lets you define AI agents with clear roles and areas of focus, ensuring Joule behaves in a way that aligns with your processes and supports users more effectively in their daily work.

And rather than just taking my word for it, you can see it in action in the following video:

For this case, I used a single Prompt:

“Using Easy Purchaser, create a PR 'ZNB', "Blog Joule", with: TG11, plant: 1710, 5 PC, date: 01/06/2026; TG12, plant: 1710, 7 PC, date: 01/06/2026, ZJoule: Demo”.

Compared with the initial video, Joule did not ask for a source of supply or whether the request was for a material or a service. It also allowed me to select the document type “ZNB”, populate the PR description, and fill the custom field “ZJoule.” This behaviour is intentional and reflects how Joule was configured in Joule Studio to align with the business process and reduce unnecessary user input.

 

Key Takeaway

SAP delivers a standard experience for Joule‑driven object creation. At the same time, you remain in control. By leveraging SAP Joule Studio, you can tailor Joule to meet your organization’s unique needs—without being limited to a fixed, predefined flow.

 

The SAP S/4HANA RIG Team is preparing a series of technical and functional blogs to walk you through some examples. The next blog will focus on the technical steps to enable this scenario and to adapt the Joule Agent to your business needs. Links will be updated here. Stay tuned!

Thank you again for reading, and I encourage you to embrace the latest innovations that come with SAP S/4HANA. If you found this blog helpful, please consider giving it a Like or sharing it with others.

I would also love to hear your thoughts and feedback—feel free to leave a comment. If you have any questions, please post them in the Q&A section using the tag SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. We will do our best to respond as quickly as possible.

 

Thanks,
Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG Team
